title: Small Bowel Obstruction from Adhesions
chief_complaint: Crampy abdominal pain, vomiting, and my belly is getting bigger. I haven't passed gas or had a bowel movement in 24 hours. I had my gallbladder removed 5 years ago.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Distended, tympanitic. Hyperactive bowel sounds (early SBO - high-pitched, rushing). Mild diffuse tenderness. No rebound. Surgical scars present (laparoscopic cholecystectomy). No hernias.
physical_exam.Rectal: Empty vault. No masses. Hemoccult negative.

hidden_data.ct_abdomen: Dilated small bowel loops >3.5cm with transition point in mid-ileum. Decompressed distal bowel. No free air. No pneumatosis. No portal venous gas. Moderate amount of free fluid.
hidden_data.abdominal_xr: Dilated small bowel with air-fluid levels. Step-ladder pattern. No free air.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Small Bowel Obstruction from Post-Surgical Adhesions
key_learning_points: SBO = most common surgical emergency of small intestine. Adhesions (60% - prior abdominal surgery), Hernias (20%), Tumors (10%), Crohn disease, Gallstone ileus, Intussusception

Symptoms: Crampy, colicky abdominal pain (q3-5 min initially → constant if ischemia). Vomiting (proximal SBO = early, distal = late, feculent). Abdominal distension. Obstipation. Hyperactive bowel sounds early, absent late (ischemia)

CT with IV contrast is gold standard: Shows transition point, degree of obstruction, signs of ischemia (thickened bowel wall, mesenteric edema, pneumatosis, portal venous gas). Closed loop obstruction is surgical emergency

Management: NPO, NG tube decompression, IV fluids (third-spacing significant in SBO). Non-operative management: 70-80% of adhesive SBO resolve within 48-72h. Gastrografin challenge: 100mL via NG, clamp 4-8h, AXR at 8h and 24h. If contrast in colon = partial SBO, likely to resolve

Surgery indications: Complete SBO (no air in colon), Failed NOM 48-72h, Signs of ischemia/strangulation, Closed loop obstruction, Incarcerated hernia. Laparoscopic lysis of adhesions if feasible. Laparotomy if dense adhesions or ischemia. Bowel resection if necrosis (resect + primary anastomosis or stoma if unstable)
